segments.title: HMS Foxglove's long Acacia-class career
segments.summary: Meet HMS Foxglove, an Acacia-class Royal Navy minesweeper launched in 1915 whose service extended to 1946.
segments.body: Foxglove is introduced as an Acacia-class Royal Navy minesweeper launched by Barclay Curle in Glasgow and commissioned in 1915. The class was designed primarily for minesweeping, placing a dangerous specialist task at the center of this small ship's original purpose. Her eventual scrapping in 1946 gives the viewer an immediate sense of an unusually long service life spanning both world wars. The opening therefore frames Foxglove as an example of how a modest auxiliary hull could remain useful for three decades.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Foxglove as an Acacia-class Royal Navy minesweeper, gives her 1915 launch and commission, and notes her 1946 scrapping.

segments.title: Foxglove's compact minesweeper hull
segments.summary: Foxglove's displacement, dimensions, and restrained bow and stern reveal the compact hull form of an Acacia-class minesweeper.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes Foxglove as a roughly 1,200-ton ship with a length varying between about 250 and 262 feet depending on the measurement used. Her beam was 33 feet and her draft about 12 feet, giving a compact set of proportions for the work expected of her. He also notes that neither the bow nor the stern projected very strongly beyond the hull's main length. This close look at basic dimensions helps viewers connect the Acacia class's minesweeping role with the scale and shape of the vessel that performed it.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Foxglove's approximate displacement, alternative length measurements, beam, draft, and the limited projection of her bow and stern.

segments.title: Designed and service armament did not always match
segments.summary: Foxglove's reported gun fits show why a warship's designed armament should not be assumed to describe every point in its career.
segments.body: The Acacia-class design called for two 12-pounder guns and two 3-pounders. Dr. Clarke cautions, however, that Foxglove did not necessarily carry that nominal battery throughout her career. At several points she appears to have mounted four-inch guns and more 3-pounders than the original specification listed. The comparison teaches viewers to distinguish a class's paper design from the changing armament fitted to an individual ship in service.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Foxglove's designed 12-pounder and 3-pounder armament with reported four-inch-gun fits and additional 3-pounders.

segments.title: From World War I minesweeping to wider wartime service
segments.summary: Foxglove spent her first two wartime years sweeping mines before moving into broader fighting duties in 1917.
segments.body: Foxglove's early World War I work was primarily minesweeping, the practical and dangerous task for which the Acacia class had been built. She spent roughly two years in that specialist employment before the Royal Navy redirected her in 1917. Dr. Clarke describes the change as a move into broader war-fighting work, although this short passage does not specify individual operations. The shift still illustrates how wartime demand could pull a purpose-built auxiliary into more general naval service.

evidence.note: The transcript says Foxglove performed minesweeping duties until 1917 before being turned to broader war-fighting work.

segments.title: China Station rescue work with HMS Carlisle
segments.summary: On the China Station, Foxglove helped HMS Carlisle rescue survivors from the grounded SS Hong Moh in 1921.
segments.body: During her 1920s China Station service, Foxglove responded to the grounding of SS Hong Moh near White Rock off Lamock Island on 3 March 1921. She worked alongside the C-class cruiser HMS Carlisle to render assistance to the passengers. Between the two Royal Navy ships, 221 survivors were rescued. The episode shows the broad peacetime utility of a small vessel on a distant station, where maritime rescue could become an immediate operational duty.

evidence.note: The transcript places Foxglove on the China Station and describes the 3 March 1921 rescue effort with HMS Carlisle, including 221 survivors.

segments.title: Damage, conversion, and service to 1946
segments.summary: German dive bombers damaged Foxglove off the Isle of Wight, but she remained afloat and continued the war in support roles.
segments.body: Foxglove's active World War II contribution was cut short when German aircraft dive-bombed and damaged her off the Isle of Wight in July 1940. She remained afloat, allowing the Royal Navy to retain the hull rather than treat the attack as the end of her usefulness. Foxglove became an accommodation and base ship and then served as a harbour guard ship at Londonderry for the rest of the war. Dr. Clarke's closing reference to her China Station and Mediterranean work emphasizes how much service preceded the damage, while her continued support employment and 1946 scrapping complete the story of a durable small warship.

evidence.note: The transcript describes dive-bomb damage off the Isle of Wight, conversion to accommodation and base-ship roles, harbour guard service at Londonderry, and 1946 scrapping.
The closing remarks mention China Station and Mediterranean service, note that active World War II work ended early in July 1940, and judge the ship a valuable build.
